A reservoir of glycerin has a mass of 1200 kg and a volume of 0.952 m
. Find the glycerin’s weight, mass density, specific weight and specific gravity.

children.html: 11.77  kN  ;1261  kg/m3  ;12.36  N/m3  ;1.2611.77 \; kN \;; 1261 \; kg/m^3 \; ; 12.36 \; N/m^3 \; ; 1.2611.77kN;1261kg/m3;12.36N/m3;1.26

children.html: W=m⋅gW= m \cdot gW=m⋅g

s=ρfluidρwater4cs= \frac {\rho_{fluid}}{\rho_{water_{4c}}}s=ρwater4c​​ρfluid​​

children.value: Click here for a video explaining the problem

%%render 2 long
m=1200 #kg
Vol=0.952 #m3
W= m*g #N
rho_glycerin= m/Vol #kg/m3
gamma_glycerin= rho_glycerin*g #N/m3
s_glycerin= rho_glycerin/rho_water

text/latex.content:
\[
\begin{aligned}
m &= 1200 \; \;\textrm{(kg)}
\\[10pt]
\mathrm{Vol} &= 0.95 \; \;\textrm{(m3)}
\\[10pt]
W &= m \cdot g \\&= 1200 \cdot 9.81 \\&= 11772.00 \; \;\textrm{(N)}\\[10pt]
\\[10pt]
\rho_{glycerin} &= \frac{ m }{ \mathrm{Vol} } \\&= \frac{ 1200 }{ 0.95 } \\&= 1260.50 \; \;\textrm{(kg/m3)}\\[10pt]
\\[10pt]
\gamma_{glycerin} &= \rho_{glycerin} \cdot g \\&= 1260.50 \cdot 9.81 \\&= 12365.55 \; \;\textrm{(N/m3)}\\[10pt]
\\[10pt]
s_{glycerin} &= \frac{ \rho_{glycerin} }{ \rho_{water} } \\&= \frac{ 1260.50 }{ 1000 } \\&= 1.26  \\[10pt]
\end{aligned}
\]

children.value:
%%render 2
p_Pa= 3500 #Pa
T_k= 30+273.15 #K
R_wvapour= 462 #m2/(s2.K)
p_barometer=102*1000 #Pa
R_air=287 #m2/(s2.K)
gamma_wvapour= (g*p_Pa)/(R_wvapour*T_k) #N/m3
The partial pressure can be calculated as

children.html: ppartial=ptotal−pvp_{partial}=p_{total}-p_{v}ppartial​=ptotal​−pv​

text/latex.content:
\[
\begin{aligned}
p_{air_{Pa}} &= p_{barometer} - p_{Pa} \\&= 102000 - 3500 \\&= 98500 \; \;\textrm{(kPa)}\\[10pt]
\\[10pt]
\gamma_{air} &= \frac{ g \cdot p_{air_{Pa}} }{ R_{air} \cdot T_{k} } \\&= \frac{ 9.81 \cdot 98500 }{ 287 \cdot 303.15 } \\&= 11.11 \; \;\textrm{(N/m3)}\\[10pt]
\end{aligned}
\]

A rigid cylinder with inside diameter of 15 mm contains a column of water 500 mm long. What will the column length be if a force of 2 kN is applied to its top by a frictionless plunger? Assume a bulk modulus of 2.21 GPa.

children.html: 497  mm497 \; mm497mm

children.value: The compressibility of liquids is described by the following equation:

children.html: Δp=−EvΔvv\Delta p =-E_v \frac{\Delta v}{v}Δp=−Ev​vΔv​

children.value: The change in pressure can be calculated using the force and area as:

children.html: Δp=ΔFA\Delta p = \frac {\Delta F}{A}Δp=AΔF​

children.value: The cylinder’s cross-sectional area is fixed, but the column length is changing, modifying the fluid’s volume

children.value: The change in pressure can be calculated using the force and area as:

children.html: Δp=ΔFA\Delta p = \frac {\Delta F}{A}Δp=AΔF​

children.value: Changing specific volume with the following equation, we have

children.html: Δvv=ΔVolmVolm\frac {\Delta v }{v}= \frac {\frac{\Delta Vol}{m}}{\frac{Vol}{m}}vΔv​=mVol​mΔVol​​

children.value: Knowing that the change in volume is due to the change in length since the area remains constant, we have the following:

children.html: Δvv=L2−L1L1\frac {\Delta v }{v}= \frac{L_2-L_1}{L_1}vΔv​=L1​L2​−L1​​

children.value: Substituting into the compressibility equation, we have:

children.html: Δp=−EvL2−L1L1\Delta p =-E_v \frac{L_2-L_1}{L_1}Δp=−Ev​L1​L2​−L1​​
L2L_2L2​
L2=−L1⋅ΔpEv+L1L_2 = -L_1 \cdot \frac{\Delta p}{E_v}+ L_1L2​=−L1​⋅Ev​Δp​+L1​

children.value: Problems for Lecture 3: Vapour pressure and surface tension

children.label: Problems for Lecture 3: Vapour pressure and surface tension

children.value: At approximately what temperature will water boil at top of Mount Everest (elevation 8 850 m above sea level)?

children.html: 70  °C70 \; °C70°C

children.value: Click here for a video explaining the problem
A fluid will boil when its saturation vapour pressure is greater than or equal to the pressure.
From the ICAO standard atmosphere table, the absolute pressure at 8850 m above sea level is 32000 Pa.
From the Physical properties of water at standard sea-level atmospheric pressure table, water has a saturation vapour pressure of 32000 Pa at 70°C.
At approximately what elevation will water boil at 90 °C?

children.html: 3000  m3000 \; m3000m

children.value: Click here for a video explaining the problem
From the Physical properties of water at standard sea-level atmospheric pressure table, water has a saturation vapour pressure of 70.1 kPa at 90°C.
From the ICAO standard atmosphere table, the absolute pressure at 70 kPa is 3000 m.

Water at 75°C is placed in a beaker in an airtight container. Air is gradually pumped out of the container. What reduction below standard sea level atmospheric pressure must be achieved before the water boils?

children.html: 62.325  kPa62.325 \; kPa62.325kPa
dp=patm−pvpdp=p_{atm}-p_{vp}dp=patm​−pvp​

children.value: Click here for a video explaining the problem
The pressure reduction can be calculated as the difference between the standard sea level atmospheric pressure and the saturation vapour pressure:

children.html: dp=patm−pvpdp=p_{atm}-p_{vp}dp=patm​−pvp​

children.value: From the ICAO standard atmosphere table, the standard sea level atmospheric pressure is 101.325 kPa. And from the Physical properties of water at standard sea-level atmospheric pressure table, the saturation vapour pressure at 75°C is 39 kPa.
Calculate the capillary rise of pure water at 10 °C expected in an 0.8 mm diameter tube.  Assume that the contact angle of pure water is 0°.

children.html: 37.8  mm37.8 \; mm37.8mm

children.value: Create a body diagram of the rising fluid.
The downward force is the weight of the fluid.
The upward force is equal to the vertical component of the surface tension multiplied by the length of the interface between the water and glass.

sigma=0.0742 #N/m

h_m= (2*sigma)/(gamma*(d/2)) #m
h_mm= h_m*1000 #mm

Fσy−W=0F_{\sigma y}-W=0Fσy​−W=0
σcosθπd=ρgπd24h\sigma cos \theta \pi d = \rho g \frac{\pi d^2}{4} hσcosθπd=ρg4πd2​h

children.value:
%%render long 4
d_tube=1.5/1000 #m
sigma=0.0730 #N/m
theta=10 #°

theta_rad = radians(theta) #rad

F_sigma= sigma*pi*d_tube*cos(theta_rad) #N
h= F_sigma*4/(d_tube**2*rho_water*g*pi) #m
h_mm = h_=h*1000 #mm

text/latex.content:
\[
\begin{aligned}
d_{tube} &= 0.0015 \; \;\textrm{(m)}
\\[10pt]
\sigma &= 0.0730 \; \;\textrm{(N/m)}
\\[10pt]
\theta &= 10 \; \;\textrm{(°)}
\\[10pt]
\theta_{rad} &= \operatorname{radians} \left( \theta \right) \\&= \operatorname{radians} \left( 10 \right) \\&= 0.1745 \; \;\textrm{(rad)}\\[10pt]
\\[10pt]
F_{\sigma} &= \sigma \cdot \pi \cdot d_{tube} \cdot \cos \left( \theta_{rad} \right) \\&= 0.0730 \cdot 3.1416 \cdot 0.0015 \cdot \cos \left( 0.1745 \right) \\&= 0.0003 \; \;\textrm{(N)}\\[10pt]
\\[10pt]
h &= F_{\sigma} \cdot \frac{ 4 }{ \left( d_{tube} \right) ^{ 2 } \cdot \rho_{water} \cdot g \cdot \pi } \\&= 0.0003 \cdot \frac{ 4 }{ \left( 0.0015 \right) ^{ 2 } \cdot 1000 \cdot 9.8100 \cdot 3.1416 } \\&= 0.0195 \; \;\textrm{(m)}\\[10pt]
\\[10pt]
h_{mm} &= h_{} \\&= h \cdot 1000 \\&= 19.5422 \\&= 0.0195 \cdot 1000 \\&= 19.5422 \; \;\textrm{(mm)}\\[10pt]
\end{aligned}
\]

children.value: A glass tube with diameter 1 mm is dipped vertically in water. Calculate from basic principles how high the water will rise in the tube due to capillary action. Take the coefficient of surface tension to be 0.0730 N/m and the angle of contact as 20°.

children.html: 28  mm28 \; mm28mm

children.value: Create a body diagram of the rising fluid.
The downward force is the weight of the fluid.
The upward force is equal to the vertical component of the surface tension multiplied by the length of the interface between the water and glass.
Click here for a video explaining the problem

from PIL import Image
img = Image.open(r"figures\L3Q4-6s.png")
img= img.resize((490,400))
img

children.value:
%%render long 3
d=1/1000 #m
sigma=0.073 #N/m
theta=20 #°
h= (4*sigma*cos(radians(theta)))/(d*rho_water*g) #m
h_mm= h*1000 #mm
A square block has a contact area of 0.02 m
with a table that is lubricated with a 0.1 mm thick layer of oil with viscosity 7 mPa.s. What force is required to move the block at a constant velocity of 1 m/s?

children.html: 1.4  N1.4 \; N1.4N

children.value: Since the block moves at constant velocity, the forces actig on it are in equilibrium.
The shear stress or the block can be calculated from the viscosity equation.
THe viscosity force on the block is the shear stress × area.
Click here for a video explaining the problem

from PIL import Image
img = Image.open(r"figures/4_viscosity_1.png")
base_width = 350 # Change this to your desired width
w_percent = base_width / float(img.size[0])
h_size = int(float(img.size[1]) * w_percent)
resized_img = img.resize((base_width, h_size))
resized_img

children.html: ∑Fx=0;Newton Ⅰ (equilibrium)\sum F_{x}=0\quad;\quad\text{Newton Ⅰ (equilibrium)}∑Fx​=0;Newton Ⅰ (equilibrium)
F−τA=0F-\tau A=0F−τA=0
τ=μdudy\tau = \mu \frac{du}{dy}τ=μdydu​

children.value: Assume a linear velocity profile in the oil

%%render long 4
A=0.02 #m2
y=0.1 #mm 
mu_oil=7*10**-3 #Pa.s
Delta_u = 1 #m/s
Delta_y= y/1000 #m
tau= mu_oil*(Delta_u/Delta_y) #Pa
F= tau*A #N
A space 16 mm wide between two large plane surfaces is filled with oil with a viscosity μ = 0.18 N.s/m
. What force F will be required to drag a very thin plate of 0.4 m
area between the surfaces at a speed of 0.25 m/s if the plate is positioned 5 mm from the bottom surface?

children.html: 5.24  N5.24 \; N5.24N

children.value: The sum of forces on the plate are in equilibrium since it is moving at a constant velocity.
The shear stress on the plate can be calculated from the viscosity equation.
The viscosity force on the plate is the shear stress × area.
The shear stress will be different on the top and bottom of the plate.
Click here for a video explaining the problem

children.html: ∑Fx=0;Newton Ⅰ (equilibrium)\sum F_{x}=0\quad;\quad\text{Newton Ⅰ (equilibrium)}∑Fx​=0;Newton Ⅰ (equilibrium)
F−Fτ1−Fτ2=0F-F_{\tau_{1}}-F_{\tau_{2}}=0F−Fτ1​​−Fτ2​​=0
F=Fτ1+Fτ2F=F_{\tau_{1}}+F_{\tau_{2}}F=Fτ1​​+Fτ2​​

children.value:
%%render long 3
mu=0.18 #N.s/m2
u=0.25 #m/s
Delta_y_2=5/1000 #m
y=16/1000 #m
A=0.4 #m2

Delta_y_1= y-Delta_y_2 #m
tau_1= mu*(u/Delta_y_1) #N/m2
tau_2= mu*(u/Delta_y_2) #N/m2
F_tau_1= tau_1*A #N
F_tau_2= tau_2*A #N
F= F_tau_1+F_tau_2 #N
A space 10 mm wide between two large plane surfaces is filled with oil with viscosity μ = 0.18 N.s/m
. A very thin plate with a 0.5 m
area is located halfway between the two surfaces. If a constant force of 10 N is applied the plate, at what speed will it be dragged out from between the surfaces?

children.html: 0.278  m/s0.278 \; m/s0.278m/s

children.value: The forces on the plate are in equilidrium since it is moving at a constant velocity.
The shear stress on the plate can be calculated from the viscosity equation.
The shear stress will act on both sides of the plate.
The viscosity force on the plate is the shear stress × area.
Click here for a video explaining the problem

F−2Fτ=0F-2F_{\tau}=0F−2Fτ​=0

children.value:
%%render long 3
A=0.5 #m2
y=10/1000 #m
mu =0.18 #N.s/m2
F= 10 #N

Delta_y= y/2 #m
u= (F * Delta_y) / (2 * A * mu) #m/s

A square block weighing 1.1 kN and with side lengths of 250 mm slides down a 20 °  incline on a film of oil 6.0 micrometre thick. Assuming a linear velocity profile in the oil, what is the terminal velocity of the block? The viscosity of the oil is 7 mPa.s.

children.html: 5.16  m/s5.16 \; m/s5.16m/s

children.value: At terminal velocity, the block is moving at a constant speed, meaning the net force acting on it is zero.

children.html: xxx

children.html: ∑Fx=0;Newton Ⅰ (equilibrium)\sum F_{x}=0\quad;\quad\text{Newton Ⅰ (equilibrium)}∑Fx​=0;Newton Ⅰ (equilibrium)
F−Wsin⁡θ=0F-W\sin\theta=0\\F−Wsinθ=0
τA=Wsin⁡θ\tau A = W\sin\thetaτA=Wsinθ
μdudy=Wsin⁡θ\mu\frac{\mathrm{d} u}{\mathrm{d} y}=W\sin\thetaμdydu​=Wsinθ
Δu=ΔyWsin⁡θμ\Delta u = \frac{\Delta y W\sin\theta}{\mu}Δu=μΔyWsinθ​

children.value:
%%render long 6
W=1.1*10**3 #N
theta=20 #°
Delta_y=6*10**-6 #m
mu=7*10**-3 #Pa.s
b=250/1000 #m

A= b**2 #m2
Delta_u= (W*sin(radians(theta))*Delta_y)/(A*mu) #m/s
A hydraulic lift consist of a 280 mm diameter ram which slides in a 280.18 mm  cylinder, the annular space being filled with oil having a kinematic viscosity of 0.00042 m
/s and a specific gravity of 0.86. If the rate of travel of the ram is 0.22 m/s, find the frictional resistance when 2 m of the ram is engaged in the cylinder.

children.html: 1553  N1553 \; N1553N

children.value: The cylinder is static and the rod moving through it.
Calculte the viscosity force on the ram.
Kinematic viscosity is given, not dynamic viscosity.
The space between the ram and cylinder is half the difference in the diameters.
Click here for a video explaining the problem

resized_img = img.resize((base_width, h_size))

children.html: τ=μdudy\tau = \mu \frac{du}{dy}τ=μdydu​
τ=μΔuΔy\tau = \mu \frac{\Delta u}{\Delta y}τ=μΔyΔu​

children.value:
%%render long 5
d_ram= 280/1000 #m
d_cyl=280.18/1000 #m
nu= 0.00042 #m2/s
s=0.86 
Delta_u=0.22 #m/s
L=2 #m

rho= s*rho_water #kg/m3
mu= nu*rho #Pa.s
Delta_y= (d_cyl-d_ram)/2 #m

tau= mu*Delta_u/(Delta_y) #Pa
A= L*pi*d_ram #m2
F= tau*A #N

A piston with diameter of 127 mm, height 152 mm and weight 93.4 N slides free in a lubricated vertical pipe. The clearance between the piston and the pipe is 0.0254 mm. If the piston decelerates at 0.64 m/s
when its speed is 6.4 m/s, what is the viscosity of the oil?

children.html: 0.0065  N.s/m20.0065 \; N.s/m^20.0065N.s/m2

children.value: Acceleration is occuring. Thus apply Newton’s Second Law.
Click here for a video explaining the problem

children.html: ∑F=ma;Newton Ⅱ\sum F = ma\quad;\quad\text{Newton Ⅱ}∑F=ma;Newton Ⅱ
Fτ−W=maF_\tau - W=maFτ​−W=ma
Fτ=μΔuΔyAF_\tau=\mu \frac{\Delta u}{\Delta y}AFτ​=μΔyΔu​A
μΔuΔyA−W=ma\mu \frac{\Delta u}{\Delta y}A-W=maμΔyΔu​A−W=ma

children.value:
%%render long 7
d=127/1000 #m
h=152/1000 #m
W=93.4 #N
Delta_y=0.0254/1000 #m
Delta_mu=6.4 #m/s
a=0.64 #m/s2

A= 2*pi*(d/2)*h #m2
m= W/g #kg

mu= ((m*a+W)/(A*Delta_mu))*Delta_y #N.s/m2
A journal bearing consists of an 80.0 mm diameter shaft in an 80.4 mm sleeve 120 mm long. The clearance space is filled with oil with a viscosity of 0.11 N.s/m
. Calculate the torque required to keep the shaft turning at 150 rpm.

children.html: 0.417  N.m0.417 \; N.m0.417N.m

children.value: The torque is calculated by multiplying the force with the distance

children.html: T=F⋅rshaftT= F \cdot r_{shaft}\\T=F⋅rshaft​

children.value: where the force can be calculated from viscosity.
Click here for a video explaining the problem

children.html: T=F⋅rshaftT= F \cdot r_{shaft}\\T=F⋅rshaft​
F=τAF=\tau A\\F=τA
τ=μΔuΔy\tau= \mu\frac {\Delta u}{\Delta y}τ=μΔyΔu​
u=wru= wru=wr
w=2πN60w= \frac {2 \pi N}{60}w=602πN​
u=rshaft⋅2⋅π⋅Nrpm60u=\frac{r_{shaft}\cdot2\cdot\pi\cdot N_{rpm}}{60}u=60rshaft​⋅2⋅π⋅Nrpm​​

children.value:
%%render long 4
d_shaft= 80/1000 #m
d_sleeve= 80.4/1000 #m
L_shaft= 120/1000 #m
mu=0.11 #Ns/m2
N_rpm=150 #rpm

r_shaft= (d_shaft)/2 #m

u= (r_shaft*2*pi*N_rpm)/60 #m/s

Delta_y= ((d_sleeve-d_shaft))/2 #m

tau= mu*u/Delta_y #Pa

F= tau*pi*(d_shaft)*(L_shaft) #N

T= F*r_shaft #N.m
